hook: You already know that squaring a number and taking a square root undo each other — that's a powerful idea. But what happens when the root isn't a square root, or when the exponent is a fraction like $\frac{1}{3}$? This unit reveals how roots and exponents are secretly the same operation, unlocking a whole new class of functions and equations.
concept: Every topic in this unit connects through one big idea: operations and their inverses. From rewriting $\sqrt[n]{x}$ as $x^{1/n}$ to composing and inverting functions, you are building a complete toolkit for working with expressions and functions that "undo" each other — formally captured by the concept of inverse operations and inverse functions.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to rewrite radical expressions like $\sqrt[3]{8}$ using rational exponents like $8^{1/3}$, making complex roots easier to manipulate.
Apply the familiar exponent rules — product, quotient, and power properties — to rational exponents so you can simplify expressions like $x^{2/3} \cdot x^{1/3}$ with confidence.
Master how to graph functions like $f(x) = \sqrt[3]{x-2}+1$ by identifying transformations including shifts, reflections, and stretches of the parent radical function.
You will learn to isolate and eliminate radicals by raising both sides to an appropriate power, while carefully checking for extraneous solutions that can sneak in.
You will learn to build new functions by chaining two functions together, evaluating and simplifying expressions like $(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))$.
Discover how to find and verify the inverse of a function algebraically and graphically, understanding that $f$ and $f^{-1}$ perfectly undo each other's outputs.
Apply advanced reasoning to solve inequalities involving radical expressions, carefully accounting for domain restrictions and sign analysis to find solution sets.
